utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Hello, Oksana! Welcome! How are you today?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Hello, Sarah. I am... good. Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Good! I'm happy to hear that. How was your weekend?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Um... weekend is... good. I go to... how to say... park.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Oh lovely! You went to the park. That's nice! Was the weather good?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Yes, weather is... nice. Sun. But cold.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Yes, it was sunny but cold. Spring is coming though! Did you go with your family?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Yes, I go with... my children. Two children. They like park very much.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: How lovely! Your children like the park. How old are they again?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: My daughter have... she have seven years. And son have four.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Oh, so your daughter is seven years old, and your son is four. In English we say 'she is seven years old', not 'she has seven years'. Do you understand?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Oh, sorry. She is... seven years old. Yes.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: That's right! Well done! And what did you do at the park?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Um... children play. I sit and... um... look.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: The children played and you sat and watched. That sounds relaxing!

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Yes, relaxing. I like it.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Did you do anything else this weekend? Maybe some shopping?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Yes! I go on shop. On Saturday.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Ah, you went to the shop on Saturday. In English, we say 'to the shop', not 'on shop'. Can you try? I went to...?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: I went... to the shop. On Saturday.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Perfect! Well done, Oksana! And what did you buy?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: I buy... um... food. For family. Bread, and... how to say... молоко...

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Milk! Молоко is 'milk' in English.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Milk, yes. Milk. And... eggs.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Bread, milk, and eggs. Wonderful! And that's actually perfect because today's lesson is about shopping and food! Are you ready?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Oh! Yes, I am ready. This is good for me, I need this.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Excellent! It will be very useful. You said you go shopping, so this will really help you. How do you find shopping in English? Is it difficult?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Yes, it is... how to say... scary. I don't understand sometimes. People speak very fast.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: I understand. Don't worry. After today, you will feel more confident. Let's start!

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Okay. Thank you, Sarah.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: So first, do you remember - what is your favourite food?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Hmm... I like... борщ. How to say in English? Red soup with... vegetables.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Ah, borscht! Yes, it's famous. We actually use the word 'borscht' in English too. It's a beetroot soup. Do you cook it often?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Yes, I cook every week. My family like very much.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Your family likes it very much. Lovely! Okay, so let's learn some food vocabulary now. Are you ready?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Yes, ready.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Great! I'm going to show you some pictures of food. I want you to tell me if you know the English word. If you don't know, that's absolutely fine - I'll teach you. Okay, what is this?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: This is... apple. Yes, apple.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Good! This is an apple. We need 'an' before 'apple' because apple starts with a vowel sound. This is an apple. Can you say that?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: This is... an apple.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Well done! And what colour is the apple?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: It is red.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: That's right! It's a red apple. Okay, next one. What is this?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: This is... bread. Хліб. Bread.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Yes! This is bread. Well done. Do you like bread?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Yes, very much. Ukrainian bread is very good.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: I've heard that! I'd love to try it. Okay, and what about this one?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Um... I don't know. White... what is this?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: This is milk. Can you say 'milk'?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Milk. Yes, I know this word, sorry. Milk.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: No need to say sorry! You're doing great. Let's continue. What is this?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: This is... hmm... сир... I don't know English word.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: This is cheese. Cheese. Can you repeat?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Cheese. This is cheese.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Well done! Cheese is a very useful word. Do you buy cheese often?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Yes, my children like cheese very much. I buy every week.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Your children like cheese very much - that's great! Okay, next picture. What is this?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Chicken! This is chicken.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Excellent! That's right, this is chicken. Very good, Oksana!

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: I cook chicken... um... yesterday. For dinner.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Oh, you cooked chicken yesterday for dinner. Lovely! Okay, what about this one?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: I think... rice? This is rice?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Yes! This is rice. Well done! Do you like rice?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Hmm... it is okay. I like more potato.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: You prefer potatoes. That's fine! So let's look at this one - what is this?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Tomato!

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Yes! A tomato! In British English we say 'tomahto' and in American English they say 'tomayto'. Both are correct. Can you say it?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: To-mah-to. Tomato.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Good! And what colour is a tomato?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Red. Tomato is red.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: That's right! Now, what about this one? You mentioned this already.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: This is... potato. I know this!

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Wonderful! This is a potato. And you said you like potatoes. Do you make Ukrainian dishes with potatoes?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Yes! Many dish with potato. Um... вареники... how to say... like small... with potato inside.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Ah, like dumplings! Filled with potato. That sounds delicious!

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Yes, dumplings! Very good. I cook for children.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Lovely! Okay, two more. What is this?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Water.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Yes! This is water. And the last one?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Um... сік... juice? Juice.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Yes! This is juice. Well done, Oksana! You actually knew most of the words. That's really impressive!

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Thank you. But I need practice more.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: You need to practise more - and that's exactly what we're doing! Now let me check - can you remember all ten words? I'll point to the pictures and you tell me. Ready?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Okay, I try.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: What's this one?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Apple. An apple.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Good - you remembered 'an'! Next?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Bread.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Yes! Next?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Milk.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Next?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Um... this is... the white one... cheese!

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Yes! Well done. Next?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Chicken.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Next?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Rice.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Next?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Tomato.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Next?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Potato!

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Next?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Water.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: And the last one?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Juice.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Fantastic! Ten out of ten! You remembered every single one. I'm very impressed!

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Oh, thank you! I am happy.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: You should be happy! Now let's practise using them in sentences. I'll say 'What is this?' and you answer with 'This is a...' or 'This is...' okay? Remember, with apple we say 'an apple' because it starts with a vowel. With the others we say 'a tomato', 'a potato'. But with milk, bread, rice, cheese, water, and juice - we don't use 'a' or 'an'. We just say 'This is milk'. Do you understand?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Um... why? Why no 'a' with milk?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Great question! Because milk, bread, rice, cheese, water, and juice - you can't count them. You can't say 'one milk, two milks'. But you can say 'one apple, two apples'. So we say 'an apple' but just 'milk'. Does that make sense?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Ah... yes. I think I understand. Apple is one, two, three. Milk is just... milk.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Exactly! You've got it! Okay, let's practise. What is this?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: This is... cheese.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: That's right! No 'a' needed. What is this?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: This is a chicken.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Good! And this one?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: This is... tomato.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Almost! Remember, tomato is countable, so we need... This is...?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: This is a tomato?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Yes! This is a tomato. Well done. And this?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: This is water.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Perfect! Okay, you're doing brilliantly. Let's move on to the next part. We're going to talk about numbers and prices.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Oh, numbers. Okay.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: So first, can you count from one to ten for me?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: One, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, nine, ten.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Excellent! And can you continue? Eleven, twelve...?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Eleven, twelve, thirteen... um... fourteen, fifteen... um... sorry, I forget.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: That's okay! After fifteen comes sixteen. Can you say 'sixteen'?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Sixteen.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Good. Seventeen?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Seventeen.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Eighteen?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Eighteen.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Nineteen?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Nine-teen.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: And twenty?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Twenty.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Well done! Let's practise those tricky ones again. Can you say eleven to twenty all together?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Eleven, twelve, thirteen, fourteen, fifteen, sixteen, seventeen, eighteen, nineteen, twenty.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Brilliant! Much better. Now, in the UK, we use pounds and pence for money. Do you know what a pound is?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Yes, pound. Like... one pound, two pound.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Almost! One pound, two pounds - with an 's' at the end when it's more than one. One pound, two pounds. Can you say that?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: One pound, two pounds.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Good! And pence is the small money. One hundred pence equals one pound. So if something costs one pound and fifty pence, we often say 'one fifty'. Like 'one pound fifty'. Do you understand?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: I think yes. Like... one pound fifty. Is one pound and half?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Exactly! One pound fifty is one and a half pounds. Very good! Now, let me teach you a very important question for shopping. 'How much is it?' Can you say that?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: How much is it?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Perfect! 'How much is it?' is the question you ask when you want to know the price. And the answer is 'It costs...' or 'It's...' For example, 'How much is the apple?' 'It costs fifty pence.' Can you try? How much is the apple?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: How much is... apple?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Good try! Just remember - 'How much is the apple?' We need 'the' before 'apple' here. Try again?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: How much is the apple?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Well done! And the apple costs fifty pence. Now let me ask you. How much is the bread?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Um... bread cost... one pound?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Good guess! And good sentence, but we need to say 'The bread costs one pound.' With an 's' on 'cost' because it's 'the bread' - it costs. Can you try the full sentence?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: The bread costs one pound.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Excellent! Now let's practise with different foods and prices. I'll tell you the price and you make the sentence. The milk costs one pound twenty. Can you say that?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: The milk costs... one pound twenty.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Good! The cheese costs two pounds fifty. Your turn.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: The cheese cost... two pounds fifty.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Almost perfect! Just remember 'costs' with an 's'. The cheese costs two pounds fifty.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: The cheese costs two pounds fifty. Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: No need to be sorry! You're learning and that's completely normal. Okay, how about this - the chicken costs three pounds. Can you say it?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: The chicken costs three pounds.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: That's right! Now I want you to ask me the question and I'll answer. Ask me about the tomatoes.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Um... how much is... the tomato?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: If we're talking about more than one tomato, we say 'How much are the tomatoes?' Because 'tomatoes' is plural - more than one. 'How much are the tomatoes?'

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: How much are the tomatoes?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: The tomatoes cost one pound fifty per kilo. Now ask me about the potatoes.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: How much are the... potatoes?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: The potatoes cost eighty pence per kilo. Well done! You're really getting the hang of this.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Thank you. It is... not easy but I try.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: You're doing wonderfully. Now, let me test you. If the apples cost one pound, and you want two kilos, how much do you pay?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Um... two pounds?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Yes! Two pounds! Can you say the full sentence? 'Two kilos of apples cost two pounds.'

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Two kilos of apples cost two pounds.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Excellent! Now, I want to teach you another useful word. When you buy things, you might want to say how many. Like 'I want three apples' or 'I want two kilos of rice'. With countable things like apples and tomatoes, we use numbers - one apple, two apples. With uncountable things like rice, bread, milk, we say 'some'. 'I want some milk.' 'Can I have some bread?' Is that clear?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: I think... yes. Some milk. Some bread. But... one apple, two apple.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Almost! One apple, two apples - with an 's'. When there's more than one, we add 's'. One apple, two apples. One tomato, two tomatoes.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Two apples. Three tomatoes. Yes?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Yes! That's right! Well done. Now, I want you to make a shopping list. Tell me five things you want to buy. Say 'I want...' and then the food.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Okay. I want... some milk. I want... bread.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Good! But remember, with bread we also use 'some'. I want some bread. Can you say it?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: I want some bread. And I want... three apple.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Three...?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Three apples! Sorry, three apples.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: That's right! Keep going.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: I want... um... chicken. And I want some... water.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Let me just check - 'I want a chicken' or 'I want some chicken'? If you want a whole chicken, we say 'a chicken'. If you want some chicken meat, we say 'some chicken'. Which one?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Um... some chicken. For cooking.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Some chicken - perfect! Great shopping list. Milk, bread, three apples, some chicken, and water. Very good! Now, let's talk about how to actually have a conversation in a shop. Are you ready?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Yes, I am ready. This is what I need most.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: I know! So when you go into a shop, the shopkeeper might say 'Hello, can I help you?' or 'What can I get you?' And you can answer 'Hello, can I have some milk please?' or 'I'd like some bread please.' Let me write the key phrases.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Okay, yes.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: So the first important phrase is 'Can I have... please?' This is very polite. 'Can I have some milk please?' 'Can I have two apples please?' Can you try? Ask me for bread.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Can I have... bread please?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Good! But remember we need 'some' with bread. 'Can I have some bread please?'

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Can I have some bread please?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Perfect! Now ask me for cheese.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Can I have some cheese please?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Brilliant! And ask me for apples - let's say five apples.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Can I have five apples please?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Excellent! Well done, Oksana! And after you ask for everything, you want to know the price, right? So you say 'How much is it?' or 'How much is that?' And then the shopkeeper will tell you. And then you pay! What do you say when you pay?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Um... I don't know. Дякую? Thank you?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Yes! You can say 'Here you go' when you give the money, and then 'Thank you' or 'Thanks'. And if you get change back, the shopkeeper might say 'Here's your change.' Let me show you the whole dialogue. I'll be the shopkeeper first.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Hello! What can I get you today?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Um... hello. Can I have... some milk please?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Of course! Anything else?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Yes, and... can I have some bread please?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Sure! Here you go. Anything else?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: No, thank you. How much is it?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: That's two pounds fifty, please.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Here you go.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Thank you! Here's your change. Have a nice day!

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Thank you! Have a nice day.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: That was wonderful! You did that so well! How did it feel?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: It was... not so scary! I think I can do this.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Of course you can! Let's do it again, but this time I want you to buy more things. Let's say you need to buy food for dinner. What would you buy?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Hmm... chicken, rice, tomato, and... juice for children.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Good! Remember - 'tomatoes' with an 's' if you want more than one. And 'juice for the children'. Okay, let's do it! Hello, welcome to Sarah's shop! What can I get you?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Hello. Can I have some chicken please?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Of course! How much chicken would you like?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Um... I don't know. How to say... enough for four people?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: You could say 'enough for four people' - that's actually perfect! Or you could say 'about one kilo'. Let me give you one kilo. Anything else?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Yes, can I have some rice please? And... four tomato.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Four tomatoes - remember the 's'! But well done for using the number.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Sorry, four tomatoes.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: No problem! Here you go. Rice and four tomatoes. Anything else?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: And juice. Orange juice for children.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Orange juice for the children. How much?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Um... one... bottle?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: One bottle of orange juice. Here you go! Is that everything?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Yes, that is everything. How much is it?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Let me see... that's seven pounds twenty, please.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Seven pounds twenty. Here you go.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Thank you very much! Here's your change. Have a lovely day!

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Thank you. Have a lovely day!

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: That was great, Oksana! You're getting more confident already. Let's take a little break now. Would you like some water?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Yes, please. Thank you. I am a little... how to say... tired? My head is full!

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Ha! That's completely normal. When you learn a lot of new things, your brain gets tired. Let's just chat for a minute. How are you finding life in the UK? You moved recently, didn't you?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Yes, I move... I moved... six months ago. It is... different. But people are nice.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: I'm glad people are nice! And your English is improving so quickly. Where do you live?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: I live in... small town. Near London. It is quiet. Good for children.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: That sounds lovely! Do you like it there?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Yes, but I miss Ukraine. I miss my... how to say... мама. My mother.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Of course, that's natural. Do you talk to her on the phone?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Yes, every day. She want to know everything about children.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: She wants to know - with an 's' on 'want' because 'she wants'. But that's lovely that you talk every day.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: She wants. Yes. It is good to hear Ukrainian. Sometimes I feel... lonely? Because I don't speak English good.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: I understand, and you speak English well - we say 'well' not 'good' for how you do something. But honestly, Oksana, your English is getting better every lesson. Really.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Thank you, Sarah. You are very kind.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: I mean it! Okay, are you ready to continue? We're going to do some fun role-plays now!

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Yes, I am ready. Let's continue.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Great! So now we're going to do a longer role-play. You are going to be the customer, and I am going to be the shopkeeper. But this time, I want you to do it more independently. I'll help if you get stuck, but try on your own first. Okay?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Okay. I will try. Don't laugh at me please.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: I would never laugh at you! I'm here to help. Okay, imagine you're walking into a supermarket. You need to buy: some milk, some cheese, six eggs, four apples, and some chicken. Ready?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Okay... some milk, cheese, eggs, apples, chicken. I try to remember.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Good morning! Welcome to our shop. How can I help you today?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Good morning. Um... can I have some milk please?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Of course! Full fat or semi-skimmed?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: I... I don't understand. Sorry. What is... semi-skimmed?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Don't worry! Full fat milk has all the cream - it's thick and rich. Semi-skimmed has less fat. Most families buy semi-skimmed. Which would you like?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Um... semi-skimmed. Please.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Here you go! One bottle of semi-skimmed milk. Anything else?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Yes, I want... can I have cheese? Some cheese.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Sure! Would you like cheddar or mozzarella?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Cheddar, please. My children like cheddar.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Good choice! Here's your cheddar. What else do you need?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: I need... eggs. Six eggs.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Six eggs. We have free-range eggs here. Here you go! Anything else?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: And four apple... apples. Sorry. Four apples.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Well done for correcting yourself! Four apples. Here you go. They're lovely and fresh today.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Thank you. And... last... can I have chicken please? Some chicken.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Of course! Would you like chicken breast or chicken legs?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Um... how to say... I want to make... soup. What is better for soup?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: For soup, a whole chicken is best, or chicken legs. They have more flavour.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Chicken legs, please. Four chicken legs.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Four chicken legs, here you go! Is that everything?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Yes, that is all. How much is it?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Let me add that up. The milk is one pound twenty, the cheese is two pounds, the eggs are one pound eighty, the apples are one pound, and the chicken legs are three pounds fifty. That comes to nine pounds fifty altogether.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Nine pounds fifty. Okay. Here you go.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Thank you! Out of ten pounds, so your change is fifty pence. Here you go. Have a wonderful day!

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Thank you very much. Have a wonderful day!

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Oksana, that was brilliant! You managed the whole conversation almost by yourself! How do you feel?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: I feel... proud? Is that right word? I am proud.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Yes! You should feel proud! That's exactly the right word. Now, shall we swap? This time, you be the shopkeeper and I'll be the customer. This is good practice for listening and responding.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Oh... um... I don't know if I can. What do I say?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Don't worry! As the shopkeeper, you just need a few phrases. First, you greet the customer: 'Hello, how can I help you?' Then when they ask for something, you say 'Here you go.' And when they ask the price, you tell them. And at the end, 'Thank you, have a nice day.' Shall I write those down?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Yes, please. I write down too.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Good idea. So: 'Hello, how can I help you?', 'Of course, here you go.', 'Anything else?', 'That's... pounds... pence, please.', 'Thank you, have a nice day!' Got it?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Yes, I think so. Okay, I try.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Great! Whenever you're ready, greet me as the shopkeeper.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Um... hello. How can I help you?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Hello! Could I have some tomatoes please? About six.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Six tomatoes. Um... here you go.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Thank you! And could I have a loaf of bread as well?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Yes... of course. Here you go. Um... anything else?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Yes, I'd like some rice please and a bottle of water.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Rice and water. Here you go. Anything else?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: No, that's everything, thanks. How much is that altogether?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Um... that is... hmm... I need think... four pounds... fifty? Four pounds fifty, please.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Here's a five pound note.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Thank you. Your change is... fifty pence. Thank you, have a nice day!

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Thank you! That was amazing, Oksana! You were a great shopkeeper!

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Really? I was very nervous.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: You didn't seem nervous at all! You used all the phrases correctly. Let's do one more where I'll be a more difficult customer - I might change my mind or ask questions. Are you okay with that?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Oh... okay. I try. But please, not too difficult.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Don't worry, I'll be nice! Okay, ready?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Hello! Welcome to... my shop. How can I help you?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Hi there! I'm looking for some apples. Do you have any?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Yes, we have apples. How many you want?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Hmm, actually, how much are they?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Apples cost... um... one pound for... four.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: One pound for four? That's a good price! I'll take eight, please.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Eight apples. Here you go. That is two pounds.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: And do you have any orange juice?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Yes, we have orange juice. It cost... it costs... um... one pound fifty.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Actually, do you have apple juice instead? I've changed my mind.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Hmm... sorry, I don't understand. You want apple juice? Not orange juice?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Yes, I changed my mind. I'd like apple juice instead of orange juice.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Okay, apple juice. Yes, we have. Here you go. It costs also one pound fifty.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Great! And can I have some cheese as well? About two hundred grams?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Two hundred grams cheese. Here you go. Cheese costs... three pounds for... hmm... I don't know. Three pounds.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: That's fine! Is that everything... let me think... yes, that's all. How much altogether?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: So... apples two pounds, juice one pound fifty, cheese three pounds. Total is... um... six pounds fifty.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Here's seven pounds.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Thank you. Change is fifty pence. Thank you very much! Have a nice day!

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Thank you! Bye!

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Oksana! That was incredible! I changed my mind about the juice and you handled it perfectly! You even added up the prices!

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Oh, thank you! I was so worried when you changed the juice. But I think... I understand.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: You did fantastically. In a real shop, customers do change their minds, so that was great practice. Now, I want to tell you about something that happens sometimes. When the shop doesn't have something, the shopkeeper says 'Sorry, we don't have any' or 'I'm sorry, we've run out of that.' Do you understand?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Run out? What is 'run out'?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: It means 'we had it before, but now it's all gone, there's none left.' For example, 'Sorry, we've run out of milk.' It means there's no milk today.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Ah, okay. Like... закінчилось. Finished.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Exactly! Run out means it's finished. Very good.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: This happen to me in shop last week. Woman say something and I don't understand. Maybe she say 'run out'. I was very... embarrassed.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Ah, that's so frustrating. But now you know! And you know what you can say if you don't understand someone? You can say 'Sorry, could you say that again please?' or 'Sorry, I don't understand. Can you speak slowly please?' These are very useful phrases.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Sorry, could you say that again please. Yes, this is very good. I need this.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Let's practise it. I'm going to say something fast and you ask me to repeat it. Ready?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Thatwillbethreepoundsfiftyplease.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Um... sorry, I don't understand. Can you... speak slowly please?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: That will be three pounds fifty, please.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Three pounds fifty. Okay! Here you go. That was good practice.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Exactly! Don't be afraid to ask people to speak slowly. British people are usually very understanding. Okay, let's move on to our final section - review and homework. We've done so much today!

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Yes, very much. My head is very full.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Ha! I'm sure it is. So let's review what we learned today. First, we learned ten food words. Can you remember them all? Let's see.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Um... apple, bread, milk, cheese, chicken, rice, tomato, potato, water, juice.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: All ten! Amazing memory, Oksana! Now, can you tell me - what's the question you ask when you want to know the price?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: How much is it?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: And the answer?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: It costs... five pounds.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Perfect! And what do you say when you want to buy something politely?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Can I have... some bread please?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Brilliant! And what do you say if you don't understand someone?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Sorry, can you speak slowly please?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Wonderful! You've remembered everything. Now, let me talk about a few things we need to practise more. First, articles - 'a', 'an', and 'the'. Remember, in English we usually need an article before a noun. 'I want an apple', not 'I want apple'. This is tricky because Ukrainian doesn't have articles, right?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Yes, we don't have this in Ukrainian. It is very confusing for me. I always forget.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: That's completely normal. It takes time. Just keep practising. Second, remember 'some' with uncountable nouns. Not 'I want milk' but 'I want some milk'. And third, plurals - remember the 's' at the end. Not 'three tomato' but 'three tomatoes'.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Some milk, three tomatoes, an apple. Yes, I try to remember.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: You're already improving! Now, let's do one final quick practice. I'm going to say some sentences with mistakes, and I want you to correct them. Ready? This is fun!

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Oh! I correct you? Okay, I try.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Okay. 'I want apple.' What's wrong?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: I want... an apple.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Well done! Next: 'I buy three tomato.'

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: I buy three tomatoes. With 's'.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Good! And what about the verb? 'I buy' or...?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Hmm... I... bought? I bought three tomatoes?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Yes, if it was in the past! 'I bought three tomatoes.' Or in the present, 'I buy three tomatoes' is fine too, depending on the context. Well done!

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: English tenses are very difficult.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: They are! But you're making great progress. One more: 'Yesterday I go on shop.'

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Um... yesterday I... went... to the shop? Yesterday I went to the shop.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: That's absolutely right! Two corrections - 'went' instead of 'go' for the past, and 'to the shop' instead of 'on shop'. You caught both! I'm so impressed.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Really? Thank you! I feel like I learn a lot today.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: You absolutely have. Now, let me tell you about your homework for this week. Are you ready to write it down?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Yes, I have paper. Okay.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Great! So, homework number one: practise the ten food words. Write each word five times and say it out loud. Apple, bread, milk, cheese, chicken, rice, tomato, potato, water, juice.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Write five times and say. Okay.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Homework number two: go to a real shop this week and try to buy at least three things using English. Say 'Can I have... please' and 'How much is it?' Don't be afraid to try!

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Oh... really? In real shop? I am scared.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: I know it feels scary, but you can do it! Start with something small. Maybe just buy some bread and milk. And remember, if you don't understand, say 'Sorry, can you speak slowly please?' You practised this today!

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Okay... I will try. Maybe I go to small shop, not big supermarket.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: That's a great idea! Small shops are perfect because the shopkeeper can take more time with you. And homework number three: write five sentences using the pattern 'I want some...' or 'I want a...' For example, 'I want some rice', 'I want an apple'. Five sentences.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Five sentences. I want some... I want a... Okay, I can do this.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: I know you can! And remember to think about countable and uncountable. 'Some' for uncountable, 'a' or 'an' for countable.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Countable, uncountable. Yes. Apple is countable, milk is uncountable.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Exactly right! Last homework - and this is a fun one. I want you to make a shopping list in English before you go to the shop. Write everything you need in English. If you don't know a word, try to look it up in a dictionary or Google Translate, and write it down.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Shopping list in English. Okay, this I like. I can do this at home.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Wonderful! So let me just quickly summarise. Today we learned: food vocabulary - ten words; numbers and prices using pounds and pence; how to ask for things politely in a shop; and how to have a shopping conversation. You did role-plays as both customer and shopkeeper. I'm really proud of you!

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Thank you, Sarah. Today lesson was very good. I feel more... how to say... добре... confident!

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Confident! Yes! And you ARE more confident. I could see it in the role-plays - by the last one you were barely hesitating at all.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: I want to practise more. When is next lesson?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Our next lesson is on Thursday. So in four days. Plenty of time to do your homework and practise! Do you have any questions before we finish?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Um... yes. How to say... when I am in shop and I want to ask where is something? Like, where is bread?

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Great question! You can say 'Excuse me, where is the bread?' or 'Where can I find the bread?' But let's cover that properly next lesson. We'll practise asking for directions inside a shop. Is that okay?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Yes, perfect! That will be very useful for me.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Brilliant! Anything else?

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: No, I think that is all. Thank you very much, Sarah. You are very patient with me.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: You're very welcome, Oksana. You're a wonderful student - you work so hard and you never give up. I really enjoy our lessons.

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Дякую... I mean, thank you. Sorry, sometimes Ukrainian comes out.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: That's perfectly natural! Don't apologise for it. Your Ukrainian is part of who you are. Okay, so remember - practise the words, go to a shop, write your sentences, and make a shopping list. I'll see you on Thursday!

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: Yes! I will do everything. I promise. See you on Thursday.

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: I believe you! Have a wonderful week, Oksana. Say hello to your children for me!

utterances.speaker: Oksana
utterances.text: I will. Thank you. Goodbye, Sarah!

utterances.speaker: Sarah
utterances.text: Goodbye, Oksana! Take care!
